coredata.dc:title: Novel voltammetric strategy for determination and electrochemical evaluation of progesterone by CPT-BDD electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Uçar, Muhlis
Levent, Abdulkadir
coredata.dc:description: In this study, electrochemical properties of progesterone at boron doped diamond (BDD) electrode surface were examined in strongly acidic medium (HClO4 and H2SO4). A fast, sensitive, selective and simple electroanalytic method has been proposed with a cathodically pre-treated (CPT-BDD) electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium. Progesterone yielded a well-defined voltammetric response in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium at +1.51 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). Using the optimal square wave-adsorptive stripping voltammetry (SW-AdSV) conditions, the oxidation peak was used to determine of progesterone in the concentration range of 0.1 to 7.1 μM. Limit of detection is 0.012 μM; the relative standard deviation value for the concentration value of 3.1 μM (n = 11) was calculated as 4.87%. The developed voltammetric method was successfully applied to serum and drug samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Progesterone
Boron-doped diamond electrode
Voltammetry
Serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pretreatment of BDD electrode
Cyclic voltammetric behavior of progesterone at CPT-BDD electrode
Effect of supporting electrolyte and pH

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was financially supported by the Scientific Research Projects Presidency of Batman University ( BTÜBAP-2019-YL-12 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SW-AdS voltammograms of 15 μM P4 in 0.5 M HClO4 on the BDD, APT-BDD and CPT-BDD electrodes. Accumulation potential/time: 0 V/60 s, SW variables: frequency, 25 Hz; step potential, 9 mV; pulse amplitude, 90 mV.
The repetitive cyclic voltammograms obtained for 20 mM progesterone in 0.5 M HClO4 medium by using CPT-BDD electrode. Dashed line: supporting electrolyte.
Cyclic voltammograms of 20 mM P4 in 0.5 M HClO4 medium at different scan rates by using CPT-BDD. Inset depicts the plot of peak current vs. scan rate and Log (peak current) vs. Log (scan rate).

SWV-AdSV curves of 20 μM progesterone obtained in the 0.5 M H2SO4 and 0.5 M HClO4 medium using CPT-BDD electrode. Dashed line, supporting electrolyte. Accumulation potential/time: 0.7 V/120 s, SW variables: frequency, 25 Hz; step potential, 9 mV; pulse amplitude, 90 mV.

SW-AdSV curves for different concentration of progesterone in 0.5 M HClO4 medium (0; supporting electrolyte, 1; 0.1 μM, 2; 1.1 μM, 3; 2.1 μM, 4; 3.1 μM, 5; 4.1 μM, 6; 5.1 μM, 7; 6.1 μM, 8; 7.1 μM). Accumulation potential/time: 0.7 V/120 s, SW variables: frequency, 45 Hz; step potential, 9 mV; pulse amplitude, 90 mV.
SW-AdSV curves for uric acid (80 μM), testosterone (80 μM) and progesterone (8 μM) in 0.5 M HClO4 medium by using CPT-BDDE. Accumulation potential/time and SW parameters as indicated in Fig. 6.

SW-AdSV curves for serum sample in the 0.5 M HClO4 medium (0; supporting electrolyte, 1; Blank serum, after standard additions of 2; 1.75 μM, 3; 2.75 μM, 4; 3.35 μM, 5; 4.75 μM, 6; 5.75 μM) Accumulation potential/time and SW parameters as indicated in Fig. 6.
Comparison of determination of progesterone with different analytical techniques.

row.entry: White meat, egg yolks and vegetables

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SMDE: static mercury drop electrode, DME: dropping mercury electrode BİFE; bismuth film electrode, nsBiFE: nanostructured bismuth film electrode, GCE/AuNPs/MnSB:glassy carbon/gold nanoparticles/manganese Schiff base, Fe3O4@GQD/f-MWCNTs/GCE: nanoparticles graphene quantum dots/functionalized multi–walled carbon nanotube/glassy carbon electrode, GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE: graphene quantum dots doped poly(sulfosalicylic acid/graphite oxide/glassy carbon electrode), GO-IMZ/GCE: imidazole-functionalized graphene oxide/glassy carbon electrode, Sn-modified GC: tin nanorods modified glassy carbon electrode, HPLC; high performance liquid chromatography, UHPLC; ultra high performance liquid chromatography, GC–MS: gas chromatography/mass spectrometry, DAD: Diode array detector.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Progesterone content and recoveries in pharmaceuticals (Progestan® soft gelatin capsule (100 mg/capsule)) obtained by SW-AdSV using CPT-BDDE.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Results are the average of three determination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the determination of progesterone in serum samples obtained by SW-AdSV using CPT-BDDE.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values reported are the average of three values independently obtained with the standard added.

head.ce:title: Novel voltammetric strategy for determination and electrochemical evaluation of progesterone by CPT-BDD electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Batman University, Faculty of Sciences and Arts, Department of Chemistry, 72100 Batman, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Batman University, Faculty of Sciences and Arts, Department of Chemistry, 72100, Batman, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, electrochemical properties of progesterone at boron doped diamond (BDD) electrode surface were examined in strongly acidic medium (HClO4 and H2SO4). A fast, sensitive, selective and simple electroanalytic method has been proposed with a cathodically pre-treated (CPT-BDD) electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium. Progesterone yielded a well-defined voltammetric response in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium at +1.51 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). Using the optimal square wave-adsorptive stripping voltammetry (SW-AdSV) conditions, the oxidation peak was used to determine of progesterone in the concentration range of 0.1 to 7.1 μM. Limit of detection is 0.012 μM; the relative standard deviation value for the concentration value of 3.1 μM (n = 11) was calculated as 4.87%. The developed voltammetric method was successfully applied to serum and drug samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Hormones are internal secretory substances produced for various purposes to control certain activities in the body. Hormones that play a messenger role between a cell or group of cells and other cells are produced in endocrine glands called duct-free glands and released directly into the bloodstream. Although the levels of the hormones in the blood are very low, they have a function regulating effect in the target organs and tissues by activating their specific receptors [1].

Progesterone (P4), like other steroids, is synthesized from pregnenolone, a derivative of cholesterol. It is a steroid hormone that is of vital importance in human and animal health. Imbalances in the level of P4 can cause some fertility problems such as infertility and menstrual disorder [2]. P4 level is a very important parameter in pregnancy and menstrual cycles. It prevents breastfeeding during pregnancy and allows the formation of milk by lowering the P4 level after birth [3]. Detection of P4 in milk or serum is very important in various application areas such as pregnancy diagnosis [4].

Many different analytical methods have been proposed in the literature for the determination of P4 in different matrices. Some of these methods are summarized in Table 1 . There are studies based on spectroscopic and chromatographic methods for the determination of hormone P4 in pharmaceuticals, biological liquids and environmental samples. In related methods, the determination of hormones from these mediums requires many separation and trace analysis techniques. Today, with the development of technology, mass spectroscopy (MS) can be successfully used in combination with gas chromatography (GC) and liquid chromatography (LC). Nevertheless, despite the high sensitivity and accuracy of LC and GC methods, they are very expensive and require specialized personnel, organic solvents and complex processes. Therefore, efforts to develop more reliable and precise electrochemical methods for the determination of P4 in clinical laboratory are continuing.

According to our best knowledge, the analytical performance characteristics of voltammetric techniques proposed for determination of P4 are summarized in Table 1. It is observed that toxic and environmental pollution-causing reagents and solvents are used in the process of modification of the working electrodes used in the methods on which the relevant electrochemical methods are based. Although modified electrodes increase the selectivity and sensitivity in electrochemical studies, the preparation of these electrodes is very difficult and time-consuming and includes many parameters with high cost.

The boron-doped diamond (BDD) electrodes have been used as an alternative to high performance electrodes in the electrochemical studies because of its inertness, hardness, high thermal conductivity and electrical conductivity [5–7]. In addition, the BDD electrode is highly preferred in this field with features such as low background current, a wide range of electrochemical potential, as well as robustness and durability in both alkaline and acidic media [5–10]. Moreover, the BDD electrode has advantages over other solid electrodes such as durability, hardness, optical properties and response parameters (time, precision and stability) [5,6,11–13].

In the electrochemical studies, the surface of the BDD electrode must be cleaned or activated in order to get reproducible and sensitive responses on the electrode surface, as is the case with other solid electrodes [13]. Impurities formed on the surface of the electrode cause a decrease or increase in the intensity of the analytical signal. So, this causes the results of the analysis to be incorrect. Since the surface of the BDD electrode is very sensitive, the mechanical cleaning process needs to be done very gently. With this cleaning technique, the electrode free from surface pollution is activated in different solutions at certain potentials depending on the working conditions. According to the literature, there are studies in which the cleaning process and activation of the BDD electrode are performed by applying potentials in the anodic direction [14–18] and cathodic direction [19–22] in different solutions.

In the investigation of the electrochemical properties of substances that can hardly oxidize such as the P4 hormone, it is seen that the BDD electrode is electro-activated by applying anodic or cathodic potentials in situ and especially in H2SO4 or HClO4 medium, and sensitive results are obtained. As can be seen in the literature, in the examination of the electrochemical properties of P4, it was observed that more sensitive oxidation signals were obtained when the electrodes were chemically modified [23–25].

In this study, using the information expressed above, a new and alternative electroanalytic method was developed for determination of P4 on BDD electrode in 0.5 M HClO4 medium as a result of cathodic pre-treatment (CPT) of the BDD electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium. The proposed method was successfully applied to serum and drug form after a simple dilution process without using any organic solvents (dimethyl sulfoxide used to dissolve P4) for determination of P4, without the need for long-time consuming separation processes or high-cost devices.

P4 (≥99%) was supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (P0130-100 mg). The stock solution of 1 × 10−2 M (P4) in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was prepared. Progestan® 100 mg soft gelatin capsules, the drug form of P4, were obtained from pharmacies in Turkey. In experimental studies, solutions of 0.5 M H2SO4, 0.5 M HCIO4, Britton-Robinson buffer (BR, 0.04 M), acetate buffer (ABS, 0.1 M) and phosphate buffer (PBS, 0.1 M) were used as supporting electrolyte solution. Buffer solutions were adjusted with 5.0 M NaOH or 5.0 M HCI. Milli-Q water (Millipore) was used in the preparation of all solutions. Solutions used in experimental studies were stored in the refrigerator at +4 °C.

Voltammetric studies were performed with an electrochemical analysis device Autolab PGSTAT128N potentiostat/galvanostat (Metrohm Autolab B.V., the Netherlands) controlled by the Nova1.11 software packet and Bioanalytical System Inc. (BAS) company's 100B/W (three-electrode electrochemical analyzer device) In the voltammetric experiments, working, reference and auxiliary electrodes, BDD (Windsor Scientific Ltd. Ø: 3 mm), Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl, MF 1063, BASI) and platinum wire (MF 1032, BASI) were used, respectively. pH measurements were performed with the Thermoscientific Orion 3-star pH meter.
Before starting electrochemical analysis, the BDD electrode was prepared for analysis every day as follows.

Before starting the experiments every day, the BDD electrode was polished with circular motions on the surface of the Al2O3 slurry on a soft surface pad (Al2O3 powder was left on the pad and water was added to this powder. With the help of slurry formed on the pad, the electrode was cleaned by making circular movements). Then, the slurry on the electrode surface was washed away with pure water.
1.
Cathodic and anodic (for comparative purposes) pretreatments of the BDD electrode were carried out at the beginning of each working day in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution for 300 s by applying either −3.0 or +3.0 V. Before each new scan, an activation procedure was applied under the same conditions for 60 s.
2.
Electrochemical determination of P4 was carried out by CV and SWV techniques.
3.
All measurements were performed under optimal conditions and repeated 3 times.
4.
SWV stripping parameters (unless otherwise indicated) were as follows: Accumulation potential: +0.7 V, accumulation time: 120 s, pulse amplitude: 90 mV; frequency: 45 Hz; step potential: 9 mV; rest period, 10 s; stirring rate, 600 rpm.

Progestan® 100 mg soft gelatin capsule was used as a pharmaceutical drug. 10 capsules were mixed in ultrasonic bath for 30 min to dissolve homogeneously in 250 mL DMSO. After adding the known amount of a Progestan® 100 mg solution to a 10 mL voltammetric cell, it was completed with a solution of 0.5 M HClO4, so that the total volume was 10 mL. For direct analysis of these prepared solutions, the voltammetric method in Section 2.2 was applied.

After the Ethics Committee report from the Batman Regional Hospital, serum samples were taken from the biochemistry laboratory of The Batman Hospital for Obstetrics and Pediatrics just before the experiments from a healthy and voluntary pregnant woman who was not taking drugs. After a 500 μL serum sample was added to a support electrolyte solution containing 9500 μL 0.5 M HClO4, the voltammetric method in Section 2.2 was applied for direct analysis. The determination and recovery studies were carried out with the standard addition method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pretreatment of BDD electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary studies demonstrated that both anodically [14–18] and cathodically [19–22] pretreated BDDE gave a suitable electrochemical response on BDD surface with pretreatment. To restore the electrode activity, the effect of two types of pretreatment (cathodic or anodic) was tested (for details on these pretreatments, see Experimental section), and SWV signals of 15 μM P4 in 0.5 M HClO4 medium were evaluated (Fig. 1 ). As can be seen from the figure, the cathodic pretreatment of the BDD electrode leads to higher voltammetric response, thus this pretreatment (by applying activation potential of −3.0 V for 300 s) was chosen in all following experiments, which was daily conducted before starting the experimental works. Before each voltammetric experiment, this pretreatment was always preceded by a short activation program (for 60 s) under the same condition (at −3.0 V) in order to guarantee the stability of the voltammetric responses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetric behavior of progesterone at CPT-BDD electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the electrochemical properties of P4 (20 mM) on the CPT-BDD electrode surface were examined by CV in 0.5 M HClO4 solution. The results obtained with the CV method showed that P4 is irreversibly oxidized at approximately +1.5 V (Fig. 2 ). In the second cycle, it was observed that the height of the analytic signal belonging to P4 decreased considerably. It indicates that CPT-BDD electrode may be passivated by reaction products.

The oxidation peaks current dependance on the scan rate (25–900 mVs−1) was studied by CV in 0.5 M HClO4 medium on CPT-BDD electrode. As shown in Fig. 3 , as the scan rate was increased the oxidation peak potentials shifted to positive values and higher signals were obtained. As presented in Fig. 3, there is a good linear relationship between the oxidation peak current (Ip) and the scan rate ( ν ) in the range from 25 to 900 mV s−1 (n = 11). The regression equation can be expressed as Ip (μA) = 0.037 v (mVs−1) + 6.47 (r = 0.995), indicating that the oxidation process is controlled by adsorption (shown in Fig. 3 inset). In addition, this finding was confirmed by analyzing the data through a plot of log Ip vs. log v , according to the following equation: log Ip (μA) = 0.543 log v (mVs−1) − 0.0503 ( r = 0.992). Considering the slope of 0.543 between the theoretical value of 0.5 and 1.0, it may be concluded that the adsorption plays the significant role on the CPT-BDD electrode surface.

Based on these data, the electrochemical pathway of P4 on the CPT-BDD electrode in 0.5 M HClO4 medium can be as follows (Fig. 4 ); there are several points in the structure of P4 that can be oxidized. From these, the cases with hydroxylation at the A4, A5, A2, B6 and D21 positions are the most likely. However, as it is known, alkenes can be oxidized into diols in a strongly acidic medium. In this study, P4 can be oxidized in two ways (I or II) in 0.5 M HClO4 medium on the CPT-BDD electrode. In the first case, as a result of the oxidation of the double bond in position A4–A5 to diol, each carbon can be oxidized by giving 1e− and can occur two hydroxyls to the structure (I). In the second case, a hydroxylation can occur at the B6 position. The radical or carbocation that will be formed by the removal of a hydrogen at B6 is mesomerically stable. In the next step, a H2O is attached to the intermediate product (radical or carbocation). As a result, a hydrogen is removed from the structure and the oxidation process can be completed by adding a hydroxyl giving with the exchange of 2e− (II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of supporting electrolyte and pH
ce:section.ce:para: At this stage of the research, the electrochemical properties of P4 were investigated in different supporting electrolytes (H2SO4, HClO4 ABS, BR, PBS) and pH mediums. It was observed that P4 was not electroactive on the CPT-BDD electrode in any medium except the strongly acidic medium. Fig. 5 shows SW-AdS voltammograms obtained in a strongly acidic (0.5 M H2SO4 and 0.5 M HClO4) medium of P4 (20 μM). The highest signals were obtained in 0.5 M HClO4 medium.

In this part of the study, the adsorptive properties of P4 (20 μM) in 0.5 M HClO4 medium on surface of CPT-BDD electrode were investigated. The effect of accumulation time and potential on electrode response for P4 solutions was examined. By keeping the accumulation potential constant at 0 V, the effect of accumulation time was investigated by changing the accumulation time in the range of 0 to 120 s in P4 solutions, the signals increased to 120 s, but no significant changes were observed afterwards. The effect of the accumulation potential was studied in the potential range from +0.0 V to +0.7 V by applying the accumulation time of 120 s in P4 solutions. The highest analytical signal was obtained at a potential of +0.7 V.

Effect of SW parameters (step potential, amplitude and frequency) was studied in which the analytical signal became most apparent under optimal accumulation conditions (given in Section 3.4). AdS voltammograms were recorded under conditions where the values of the other two parameters were constant for each SW parameter.

Experiments were carried out in the 0.5 M HClO4 medium of P4 (20 μM), by using the CPT-BDD electrode in conditions of step potential (0.001–0.01 V), amplitude (0.1–0.09 V) and frequency (10–70 Hz). When evaluating the results of the experiment, accumulation potential = +0.7 V, accumulation time = 120 s, frequency = 45 Hz, step potential = 9 mV and amplitude = 90 mV were selected as the best SW parameters in terms of analytical signal shape and intensity. In subsequent experimental studies, these optimal values were used.

Analytical working range, sensitivity, repeatability and selectivity of the method have been investigated by using optimal experimental parameters in order to determine the analytical performance of the voltammetric method developed on the CPT-BDD electrode. The oxidation peak current at a potential of +1.51 V increased continually with the concentration of P4 (in the range of 0.1–7.1 μM, Fig. 6 , inset) to yield a highly linear calibration plot; Ip (μA) = 1.213 C (μM) − 0.393 ( r = 0.991, n = 8), where Ip is the oxidation peak current, C the P4 concentration, r the correlation coefficient, and n the number of experiments.

The limit of detection and limit of quantification values of the proposed voltammetric method were calculated according to the LOD: 3 s/m and LOQ: 10 s/m formulas, respectively. The “s” in equation is the standard deviation of the nine signal responses that can be read on the base line of the supporting electrolyte solution, while the “m” is the slope of the calibration curve. LOD and LOQ values were found to be 0.012 (3.77 μg L−1) and 0.04 μM, respectively. Electrochemistry-based studies are summarized in Table 1. The determination of P4 in biological fluids and drugs was carried out with electrodes in the reduction-based process of DME (LOD:0.2 nM) [26], (LOD:20 nM) [27], GCE (LOD:500 nM) [28], BiFE (LOD:180 nM) [29], GO-IMZ/GCE (LOD:68 nM) [30], Sn/GCE (LOD:120 nM) [32], and electrodes in the oxidation-based process of GCE/AuNPs/MnSB (LOD:11.4 nM) [23], GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE (LOD:0.31 nM) [25] and Fe3O4@GQD/f-MWCNTs/GCE (LOD:2.18 nM) [24]. By comparing the results in the literature and the results obtained in this study, it seems that the LOD value of the SW-AdSV method that were developed using the CPT-BDD electrode for determination of P4 is lower than that of some electrochemical methods outlined in Table 1.

The precision of the developed voltammetric method was tested with the intra-day and inter-day relative standard deviation (RSD) of the oxidation peak current and potential values. SW-AdS voltammograms were recorded seven times under optimum conditions on the same day and on different days in five different solutions (4.1 μM P4). RSD% values of oxidation peak current and potential of P4 were calculated as 4.87% and 1.24%, respectively. According to the results, it can be said that the repeatability of the oxidation peak current and potential values is very good.

The selectivity study of the proposed method was performed by keeping the 8 μM P4 concentration constant and taking 10 times higher concentrations of substances that are likely to interfere. It was observed that there was not any change on the oxidation peak current and potential values of P4 in the presence of 10 times higher the concentration of Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Na+, NO3−, Cl−, uric acid, ascorbic acid, dopamine, adrenaline and noradrenaline. As seen in Fig. 7 , the peak characteristic of testosterone occurs at the potential of +1.49 V. Therefore, we can say that progesterone and testosterone cannot be simultaneous determined under the optimum experimental conditions. Thus, it can be said that the CPT-BDD electrode used in the proposed method for determination of P4 in real samples is reasonably selective.

The developed SW-AdSV method was applied to pharmaceutical and serum samples in order to verify the performance characteristics in real samples and to demonstrate its applicability. 28 soft gelatin capsules of Progestan ® 100 mg taken from pharmacies were used in the application of the method to commercial drug form. Experiments were carried out as specified in Section 2.3. Upon comparing voltammograms of the tablet samples and the standard P4 voltammograms, it was observed that the curves were compatible with each other. Quantifications were performed by means of calibration curve method. In addition, a recovery study was carried out for the accuracy of the developed voltammetric method. The results given in Table 2 demonstrated that the proposed voltammetric method could be successfully applied to pharmaceutical drug forms.

Since the reproducibility, selectivity and sensitivity of the voltammetric method developed on the CPT-BDD electrode for the determination of P4 in real samples was very good, the applicability of proposed method in complex matrice mediums such as serum samples has been investigated. Following the path described in Section 2.3, serum samples (diluted with the selected supporting electrolyte without using any complex pre-treatment and separation processes) were analyzed under optimal conditions.

As clearly seen in Fig. 8 , it is observed that P4 can be selectively determined in serum samples with CPT-BDD electrode in 0.5 M HClO4 medium. It is clearly shown that the observed peak at about +1.51 V is assigned to the oxidation of P4 since this peak height increases while adding P4 standard solution. To check the accuracy of the proposed method, the spike/recovery experiments were also performed. It is worth to mention that the level of P4 in humans may vary depending on the physiological status of these people. In the serum sample used in this study (a woman who was about 30 weeks pregnant), the level of P4 was found to be 59.7 μg L−1 using multiple standard addition method. The results outlined in Table 3 demonstrated that, the agreement between P4 detected in serum samples and addition of the P4 concentration to these samples indicates that the proposed method can determine P4 with an average recovery of 96.2% in serum samples of P4.

Looking at the studies summarized in Table 1, different analytical methods have been proposed for the determination of P4 in real samples. It is known that spectroscopic and chromatographic methods are quite troublesome and expensive, while electrochemical methods are simple and cheap. In addition, it appears that the electrodes used in the voltammetric methods presented in the literature were modified to investigate the electrochemical properties and determination of P4. In this study, a new and alternative electroanalytic method was developed on the CPT-BDD electrode surface in 0.5 M HClO4 medium as a result of cathodic electrochemical activation of the BDD electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium. The proposed method has successfully been applied to real samples after a simple dilution process without using any organic solvents, long-time separation processes or high-cost devices. Activation process applied to BDD electrode [13,17–21,39] increases the sensitivity of the method. This activation process is very fast, cheap, simple and not harmful to the environment as it does not require toxic chemicals. Furthermore, looking at the LOD values in Table 1, which summarizes the electrochemical studies proposed for the determination of P4 in serum and pharmaceutical drug forms, it seems that the LOD results obtained with CPT-BDD electrode (LOD:12 nM) are equivalent or better. The accuracy, sensitivity, repeatability, applicability and selectivity of this developed method are demonstrated by the results obtained. Additionally, the SW-AdSV method developed in this study is fast, precise, and does not require any pre-concentration process, thus it is economical, sensitive, along with working with a small amount of sample and together with its advantages such as analysis without the need for time-consuming processes. It can be presented as an alternative to chromatographic and spectroscopic methods that using more expensive, more complex and high operating costs devices.

We hope that the information that is gathered from the results of the experiment will contribute in following researches in the way of lightening the mechanism of metabolic changes on the biological systems.
Muhlis UÇAR: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation. Abdulkadir LEVENT: Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by the Scientific Research Projects Presidency of Batman University (BTÜBAP-2019-YL-12).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.W. Norman, G. Flitwick, 1997. Hormones, 2nd Edition, Academic Press, California, USA.
P.L. Senger, 2012. Pathways to Pregnancy and Parturition, 3RD Edition, Washington State University Pullman, USA.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of progesterone in whole blood samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Ehrentreich-Forster, F.W. Scheller, F.F. Bier, Detection of progesterone in whole blood samples, Biosens. Bioelectron. 18 (2003) 375–380, doi:10.1016/s0956-5663(02)00145-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Incidence of ovarian dysfunction in post partum dairy cows and the effectiveness of its clinical diagnosis and treatment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.J. McLeod, M.E. Williams, Incidence of ovarian dysfunction in post partum dairy cows and the effectiveness of its clinical diagnosis and treatment, Vet. Rec. 128 (1991) 121–124, doi:10.1136/vr.128.6.121.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electroanalysis of pharmaceuticals on boron-doped diamond electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.P. Sousa, F.W.P. Ribeiro, T.M.B.F. Oliveira, G.R. S. Banda, P. de Lima-Neto, S. Morais, A.N. Correia, Electroanalysis of pharmaceuticals on boron-doped diamond electrodes, Chem Electro Chem 6 (2019) 2350–2378, doi:10.1002/celc.201900500.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical modification of boron-doped diamond electrodes for applications to biosensors and biosensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Svítková, T. Ignat, Ľ. Švorc, J. Labuda, J. Barek, Chemical Modification of Boron-Doped Diamond Electrodes for Applications to Biosensors and Biosensing, Crit. Rev. Anal. Chem. 46 (2016) 248-256, doi:10.1080/10408347.2015.1082125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on surface termination of boron-doped diamond electrodes under anodic polarization in H2SO4 by means of dynamic impedance technique

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ryl, L. Burczyk, R. Bogdanowicz, M. Sobaszek, K. Darowicki, Study on surface termination of boron-doped diamond electrodes under anodic polarization in H2SO4 by means of dynamic impedance technique, Carbon, 96, (2016) 1093-1105, doi:10.1016/j.carbon.2015.10.064.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltammetric protocol for reliable determination of a platelet aggregation inhibitor dipyridamole on a bare miniaturized boron-doped diamond electrochemical sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Sarakhman, S. Pysarevska, L. Dubenska, D. M. Stanković, P. Otřísal, A. Planková, K. Kianičková, Ľ. Švorc, Voltammetric protocol for reliable determination of a platelet aggregation inhibitor dipyridamole on a bare miniaturized boron-doped diamond electrochemical sensor, J. Electrochem. Soc. 166 (2019) B219-B226, 10.1149/2.0381904jes.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical determination of natural drug colchicine in pharmaceuticals and human serum sample and its interaction with DNA

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advanced sensing performance towards simultaneous determination of quaternary mixture of antihypertensives using boron-doped diamond electrode
